summary: Gruber dismantles pre-earnings-call analyses of Apple that he considers logically incoherent. He targets a HotHardware piece that frames record iPhone sales at Verizon as bad news because only half were iPhone 5s, and a MarketWatch listicle demanding Apple reveal future products during an analyst call. Gruber argues that the iPhone 4 being free-on-contract at Verizon for the first time is the obvious explanation for the sales split, and that a $299 no-contract iPhone would be a natural incremental step. He defends the iPad Mini's market position despite its non-retina display, arguing it likely outsold all competing 7-inch tablets combined. The overarching point is that financial analysts and tech pundits routinely twist record-setting Apple results into doom narratives through tortured logic.
key_points: iPhone outsold all Android phones 2-to-1 at Verizon with record sales, yet pundits framed the iPhone 5 being only half of sales as a negative signal
The iPhone 4 being the first free-with-contract iPhone on Verizon is the simplest explanation for the sales split, not declining iPhone appeal
A $299 no-contract iPhone would be an incremental step from free-with-contract models, not a radical departure for Apple
Apple has never revealed upcoming products during analyst calls, making demands for iPhone 6 or Apple TV details absurd
The iPad Mini likely outsold all competing 7-inch tablets combined despite lacking a retina display and costing more, undermining the 'overpriced' narrative
money_quotes: Apple can't win by logic like this.
2007 called, and they want their 'Apple needs to make an iPhone with a keyboard' argument back.
When Arends plays poker, does he ask to see his opponents' cards?
It's like telling a restaurant that their meals cost too much and but they should use more expensive ingredients.
Arguing that the iPad Mini is (a) too expensive; and (b) should have a retina display; makes less sense than anything else Arends has argued so far, and that's saying a lot.
Imagine how well Apple could do in this category if they were 'serious' about it.
If almost everyone in the U.S. who wants an iPhone has an iPhone, why do U.S. iPhone sales continue to grow?
key_insight: Financial analysts and tech pundits consistently construct doom narratives around Apple by applying contradictory logic to record-setting results, demanding simultaneously that products be cheaper and better while ignoring that Apple is already winning the market they claim it's losing.
